macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Blanket Prohibition on Purchasing Animals from Non-Jews and the Dama ben Netinah Story

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the daf and recap of yesterday's discussion on Dama ben Netinah
Rabbi Eliezer's position on the Para Aduma and the verse 'Daber el Bnei Yisrael veyikchu'
The Gemara's question: How could they buy from Dama if the verse says 'Bnei Yisrael'?
Resolution through a Jewish middleman (tagrei Yisrael): The case of the Para Aduma purchased from Dama

macro_segments.title: Two Concerns of Rabbi Eliezer: Textual Restriction and Acts of Bestiality

micro_segments.title: The Gemara challenges Rabbi Eliezer: Is he not concerned with acts of bestiality?
Rabbi Eliezer's dual concerns (tartei): textual restriction and concern for bestiality acts
Why Rabbi Eliezer requires both a mashgiach and a middleman for the Para Aduma

macro_segments.title: The Question of Bestiality During Pregnancy and Generational Transmission

micro_segments.title: Concern: What if the mother had bestiality while pregnant? Does it transmit to the fetus?
Rava's principle: A fetus gores or is defiled together with its mother
The question of whether invalidity passes to offspring when the mother is non-sanctified
Two interpretations of the debate: Rav Nachman vs. Rav Huna bar Chinena on when invalidity transmits
Practical implication: Did they watch the animal from conception or from birth?

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Identifying and Securing a Perfect Red Heifer Through Selective Breeding

micro_segments.title: Extended concern about the mother's past: How far back must we investigate for prior bestiality?
Rav Kahana's explanation: A red cup held before the animal during conception ensures red offspring
The difficulty: If the red cup trick works, why were red heifers so expensive?
Resolution: Achieving a perfectly red heifer without any black hairs requires good genetics (muchzeket)

macro_segments.title: Challenges to Rabbi Eliezer's Position from Verses and Historical Cases

micro_segments.title: Setting: Rav Ami and Rav Yitzchak Nappacha discuss Rabbi Eliezer's position on all sacrifices
Verse: 'All the flocks of Kedar will be gathered to you' – how does Rabbi Eliezer answer this?
Rabbi Eliezer's response: In the messianic future, all nations will convert and observe the mitzvot
Rav Yosef's challenge: Does 'all worship God with one shoulder' really prove conversion to Judaism?
Alternative interpretations of when Yisro and the Amalekites brought sacrifices

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Limitation: An Age Threshold for Animals (Under Three Years Old)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's principle: There is a boundary (gevul) to the permission to buy from non-Jews
Animals under three years old: Safe because non-Jews won't perform bestiality (making them infertile)
Animals three years and older: Forbidden because non-Jews will perform bestiality at that age
Challenge to this position: Verses show non-Jews providing animals with no age restriction mentioned

macro_segments.title: The Problem of the Philistine Cows: Already Bearing Young, Thus Over Three Years Old

micro_segments.title: The Gemara raises: The Philistine cows had offspring – proving they were over three years old

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Limitation: An Age Threshold for Animals (Under Three Years Old) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Response: All those cases involved animals under three years old

macro_segments.title: The Problem of the Philistine Cows: Already Bearing Young, Thus Over Three Years Old (continued)

micro_segments.title: Mishna: A cow or donkey giving birth by its third year is definitely a bechor
Logical problem: If cows don't conceive before three years, how can these cows have calves?
Resolution: This was a special hora'at sha'ah (temporary divine ruling) and not the regular law
Tangent: Tosafot on changing nature (nishtanah hateva) – did the world change or did the Sages err?

macro_segments.title: Midrashic Interpretation: What Song Did the Philistine Cows Sing When Bringing the Ark?

micro_segments.title: Vayisharnah: The cows opened their mouths and sang as they brought the Ark
Dispute among Tannaim: Which song did they sing – about God's glory, destruction of enemies, or praise?
Rabbi Yitzchak Nappacha's version: Roni roni hashitah – a song of the Ark's glory
Rav Ashi's clarification: Rabbi Yitzchak's song was sung by Israel, not the cows
Tangent on Persian etymology: How the Persians call a book dvir and a menstruant woman dashtana

topical_tags.term: Dama ben Netinah (Story of Righteous Non-Jew)
Acts of Bestiality (Concern with Non-Jewish Animals)
Rava's Principle (Fetus & Mother as One Unit)
Rav Nachman vs. Rav Huna bar Chinena (Transmission of Invalidity)
Ger Toshav (Resident Alien with Noahide Obligations)
Sheva Mitzvot Bnei Noach (Seven Noahide Laws)
Rav Ashi (Clarifies Cows vs. Israelites Singing)
